title: Panzanella

overview.description: A Tuscan bread salad of ripe tomatoes, stale bread, red onion and basil dressed with olive oil and vinegar.

ingredients: 400g stale rustic bread, such as sourdough or Greek village bread
600g ripe tomatoes, cut into large chunks
1 small red onion, very thinly sliced
1 small garlic clove, finely grated, optional
½ tsp salt, or to taste
method: Tear the stale bread into large bite-sized pieces.
If the bread is not sufficiently stale, leave it uncovered for several hours or dry it briefly in a low oven.
Place the tomatoes in a large bowl.
Season with the salt and leave for 10–15 minutes so they release some of their juices.
Add the bread to the bowl.
Pour over the olive oil and red wine vinegar.
Toss thoroughly so the bread absorbs the tomato juices and dressing.
Leave to stand for 20–30 minutes, tossing occasionally.
Taste and adjust the oil, vinegar and salt before serving.

learning.did-you-know: Panzanella is traditionally associated with Tuscany and is a classic example of cucina povera, where simple ingredients and leftover bread are transformed into a satisfying meal.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How stale bread can absorb flavour and moisture to become the foundation of a complete salad rather than simply being served alongside it.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A particularly useful recipe for a household that regularly makes bread. It turns stale bread into a substantial meal while showcasing the flavour of good tomatoes.
chapman-tips: Good tomatoes are the most important ingredient.
The bread should be stale and firm enough to absorb dressing without instantly becoming mush.
Allow the tomatoes to release their juices before adding the bread.
Don't rush the resting time; it allows the bread to absorb the flavours properly.
The finished bread should be moist and flavourful but still retain some texture.
Use good olive oil because there are very few ingredients to hide behind.
Add the basil towards the end so it remains fresh and aromatic.
For a more substantial meal, add white beans or serve with mozzarella.
notes.batch-cooking: Better fresh. The bread continues absorbing liquid and becomes increasingly soft as it stands.
notes.leftovers: Best eaten the same day. Any leftovers can be used as a filling for toasted sandwiches or served alongside grilled food.
